perturbation.original_text: 5. The Contr actor shall promptly advise the State in writing if it learns of any unauthorized use, misappropriation, or disclosure of the Confidential Information by any of the Contractor’s Personnel or the Contractor’s former Personnel. Contractor shall, at its own e xpense, cooperate with the State in seeking injunctive or other equitable relief against any such person(s).
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The Contractor shall advise the State in writing within 30 days if it learns of any unauthorized use, misappropriation, or disclosure of the Confidential Information by any of the Contractor’s Personnel or the Contractor’s former Personnel. Contractor shall, at its own expense, cooperate with the State in seeking injunctive or other equitable relief against any such person(s).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly' to a specific 30-day period for reporting unauthorized disclosures may violate Maryland's data breach notification law, which requires notification 'without unreasonable delay'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law requires notification 'without unreasonable delay' following the discovery of a data breach. A fixed 30-day period could be deemed an unreasonable delay depending on the circumstances of the breach.

(a) In this section: (1) “Breach of the security of a system” means the unauthorized acquisition of computerized data that compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of the personal information maintained by a business; and (2) “Breach of the security of a system” does not include the good faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agent of a business for the purposes of the business, provided that the personal information is not used or subject to further unauthorized disclosure. (b) (1) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains computerized data that includes personal information of an individual residing in the State, when it discovers or is notified that it incurred a breach of the security of a system, shall conduct in good faith a reasonable and prompt investigation to determine the likelihood that personal information of the individual has been or will be misused as a result of the breach. (2) Subject to subsection (c)(4) of this section, unless the business reasonably determines that the breach of the security of the system does not create a likelihood that personal information has been or will be misused, the owner or licensee of the computerized data shall notify the individual of the breach. (3) Except as provided in subsection (d) of this section, the notification required under paragraph (2) of this subsection shall be given as soon as reasonably practicable, but not later than 45 days after the business discovers or is notified of the breach of the security of a system. (4) If after the investigation required under paragraph (1) of this subsection is concluded, the business determines that notification under paragraph (2) of this subsection is not required, the business shall maintain records that reflect its determination for 3 years after the determination is made. (b) (1) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains computerized data that includes personal information of an individual residing in the State, when it discovers or is notified that it incurred a breach of the security of a system, shall conduct in good faith a reasonable and prompt investigation to determine the likelihood that personal information of the individual has been or will be misused as a result of the breach. (2) Subject to subsection (c)(4) of this section, if, after the investigation is concluded, the business determines that the breach of the security of the system creates a likelihood that personal information has been or will be misused, the owner or licensee of the computerized data shall notify the individual of the breach. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'promptly' with a fixed 30-day period for reporting unauthorized disclosures of confidential information. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this 30-day period could be deemed an unreasonable delay, potentially violating Maryland's data breach notification law which requires notification 'without unreasonable delay'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 support this, specifically stating that a business 'shall conduct in good faith a reasonable and prompt investigation'. While Snippet 1 also states that notification should not be later than 45 days, this does not negate the 'reasonable and prompt' requirement, and a 30-day blanket period may not always be considered prompt.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The Contractor shall, at its own expense, return to the State all Confidential Information in its care, custody, control or possession upon request of the State or on termination of the Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The Contractor shall, at its own expense, return to the State all Confidential Information in its care, custody, control or possession within 90 days of request of the State or within 90 days of termination of the Contract.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of confidential information until 90 days after the state's request or contract termination may conflict with the duty to protect state information immediately upon request or termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Code, State Finance and Procurement, § 11-106
perturbation.law_citation: MD Code, State Finance and Procurement, § 11-106
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section does not explicitly mention a return timeframe, it emphasizes the contractor's responsibility for the custody, control, and preservation of State property. Introducing a 90 day delay could be seen as conflicting with these responsibilities.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the contractor to return confidential information upon the state's request or contract termination. The changed text introduces a 90-day delay. The Law Explanation indicates that this delay could conflict with the contractor's responsibility for the custody, control, and preservation of State property. Since the original contract stipulates that the contractor must return the confidential information upon request or termination, the modified text introduces a contradiction by allowing a 90 day delay.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The Contractor shall not, without the State’s prior written consent, copy, disclose, publish, release, transfer, disseminate, use, or allow access for any purpose or in any form, any Confidential Information except for the sole and exclusive purpose of performing under the Contract. The Contractor shall limit access to the Confidential Information to the Contractor’s Personnel who have a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information in order to perform under the Contract an d who are bound by confidentiality obligations at least as restr ictive as those set forth in this Agreement. Contractor shall provide copies of such agreements to the State. The names of such personnel are attached hereto and made a part hereof as Attach ment I-2. Contractor shall update Attachment I-2 by adding additional names (whether Contractor’s Personnel or a subcontractor’s personnel) as needed, from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Contractor shall not, without the State’s prior written consent, copy, disclose, publish, release, transfer, disseminate, use, or allow access for any purpose or in any form, any Confidential Information except for the sole and exclusive purpose of performing under the Contract. The Contractor shall limit access to the Confidential Information to the Contractor’s Personnel who have a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information in order to perform under the Contract and who are bound by confidentiality obligations that they agree to, whether they sign an NDA or not. Contractor does not need to provide copies of such agreements to the State. The names of such personnel are attached hereto and made a part hereof as Attachment I-2. Contractor shall update Attachment I-2 by adding additional names (whether Contractor’s Personnel or a subcontractor’s personnel) as needed, from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for confidentiality agreements and stating that they do not need to be provided to the state contradicts the requirement to ensure that personnel are bound by confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Code, State Finance and Procurement, § 11-205
perturbation.law_citation: MD Code, State Finance and Procurement, § 11-205
perturbation.law_explanation: By not ensuring legally binding agreements with personnel, the Contractor puts the State in a position where it may be unable to appropriately defend its data. Changing the text to say that the state does not need to receive the agreements and the individuals do not need to sign an NDA could be interpreted as violating the duties imposed.

(a) This section is broadly applicable to all procurements by the State. (b) A person who, for the purpose of defrauding the State, acts in collusion with another person in connection with the procurement process is liable for damages equal to 3 times the value of the loss to the State that is attributable to the collusion. (c) (1) A procurement officer who has reason to believe that collusion has occurred in connection with the procurement process shall send to the Office of the Attorney General written notice that states the belief and its basis. (2) The Attorney General may begin an action in the appropriate court for damages under subsection (b) of this section. (d) (1) If there is reason to believe that collusion has occurred in connection with the procurement process, all relevant documents shall be kept until the Attorney General gives notice that they may be destroyed. (2) On request by the Office of the Attorney General, relevant documents shall be made available immediately. Previous Next Validation Please fix the following: OK Click to return on the top page
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for confidentiality agreements and providing copies to the State. This directly contradicts the need to ensure personnel are bound by confidentiality obligations, as stated in the perturbation explanation. Without legally binding agreements, the State's data protection is weakened, potentially violating duties imposed. The law snippet 1 discusses collusion and defrauding the state, which while not directly related to confidentiality agreements, underscores the importance of enforceable obligations to protect state interests in procurement, which would be undermined by not having signed confidentiality agreements. The modified text weakens the legal protections around confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Contractor acknowledges that any failur e by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accor dingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor or any of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Contractor acknowledges that any failure by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accordingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information, but only after a 90-day notice period. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor or any of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a 90-day notice period before the State can seek an injunction against the contractor for disclosure of confidential information may conflict with the State's ability to immediately protect its information and prevent irreparable harm.
perturbation.law_explanation: This rule permits temporary restraining orders (TROs) without notice in situations where irreparable harm is likely to occur before the adverse party can be heard. By contractually requiring a 90-day notice period before seeking an injunction, the agreement contradicts the State's ability to obtain immediate injunctive relief as provided under Maryland law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Message - Maryland Code and Court Rules This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The change introduces a 90-day notice period before the State can seek an injunction. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the State's ability to obtain immediate injunctive relief where irreparable harm is likely to occur. Since the altered text requires a 90 day delay that impairs the state's ability to prevent 'irreparable harm' the contract modification contradicts the state's power to seek a TRO. Although the scraped snippets are missing, the Law Explanation provides sufficient basis to support the claim of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. The parties further agree that: a. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Maryland; b. The rights and obligations of the Contractor under this Agreement may not be assigned or delegated, by operation of law or otherw ise, without the prior written consent of the State; c. The State makes no representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information; d. The invalidity or unenforceability of any provision of this Agreement shall not affect the validity or enforceability of any other provision of this Agreement; Attac hment I – Non-Disclosure Agreement (Contractor) Page I-3 effective date: April 17, 2018 e. Signatures exchanged by facsimile are effective for all purposes hereunder to the same extent as original signatures; f. The Recitals are not merely prefatory but are an int egral part hereof; and g. The effective date of this Agreement shall be the same as the effective date of the Agreement entered into by the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The parties further agree that: a. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware; b. The rights and obligations of the Contractor under this Agreement may not be assigned or delegated, by operation of law or otherwise, without the prior written consent of the State; c. The State makes no representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information; d. The invalidity or unenforceability of any provision of this Agreement shall not affect the validity or enforceability of any other provision of this Agreement; Attachment I – Non-Disclosure Agreement (Contractor) Page I-3 effective date: April 17, 2018 e. Signatures exchanged by facsimile are effective for all purposes hereunder to the same extent as original signatures; f. The Recitals are not merely prefatory but are an integral part hereof; and g. The effective date of this Agreement shall be the same as the effective date of the Agreement entered into by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware introduces potential conflicts. Maryland law dictates requirements for contracts with the state of Maryland. Choosing Delaware law may violate those requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Code, State Finance and Procurement, § 11-104
perturbation.law_citation: MD Code, State Finance and Procurement, § 11-104
perturbation.law_explanation: This section states that contracts must comply with all applicable state laws. Choosing Delaware as the governing law may mean the contract does not adhere to relevant Maryland laws.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Maryland law, while the changed text specifies Delaware law. The LLM-provided law explanation states that contracts must comply with all applicable state laws, and choosing Delaware as the governing law may mean the contract does not adhere to relevant Maryland laws. The Perturbation explanation also indicates that changing the governing law to Delaware introduces potential conflicts, because Maryland law dictates requirements for contracts with the state of Maryland, and choosing Delaware law may violate those requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that there are Statutes for Maryland. Therefore, by changing the contract to fall under Delaware laws, it contradicts the need to comply with Maryland state laws.
